children.name: Pardalotus
children.description: A genus of tiny, brightly coloured Australian passerines known as pardalotes, with short tails, stout bills, and dazzling spotted and striped plumage patterns. They are among the smallest birds in Australia, yet their rhythmic, bell-like songs are remarkably carrying. They are largely arboreal, feeding on insects and lerps in the canopy of eucalypts.

children.name: Pardalotus punctatus
children.description: A tiny, vividly coloured bird with a black-and-white spotted crown, a yellow throat, and a crimson rump, found across much of Australia. It feeds high in eucalypt foliage, taking lerps and small insects, and is famous for its persistent, bell-like 'sleep-may-be' call. It nests in tunnels excavated in soft earth banks.

children.name: Pardalotus striatus
children.description: The most widespread and variable pardalote, with a bold white eyebrow, a yellow or orange throat, and finely streaked wings, occurring across all of Australia. Its simple, repetitive 'tink-tink-tink' call is a familiar sound in eucalypt woodlands. It nests in tree hollows, crevices, or excavated burrows, often lining the nest with strips of bark.

children.name: Pardalotus quadragintus
children.description: A critically endangered pardalote with a dull olive-green back and a distinctive pale eye-ring, endemic to Tasmania and the Bass Strait islands. Its name derives from the approximately forty white spots on its wings. It depends heavily on Eucalyptus viminalis, the manna gum, feeding on its lerps and nesting in tree hollows, and is threatened by habitat clearance and competition from other birds.

children.name: Pardalotus rubricatus
children.description: A striking pardalote with a bright red eyebrow stripe, a yellow throat, and a black-spotted crown, found in dry woodlands and shrublands across northern and central Australia. It is the largest pardalote species and the most arid-adapted, often found in mulga and acacia scrub. Its call is a clear, high-pitched piping.
